narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their early and later school grades, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPAs, family income, and whether they plan full-time studies—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it calculated a 93.3% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is why your application was denied; schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar right away. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing, signaling to the model a risk of struggling with the rigors ahead. Your Year 1 decile, also at the lowest level versus the average, reinforced this concern as the second strongest influence toward a failure prediction. Coming in third, your LSAT score of 31 was notably below the dataset average of about 37, further tipping the scales against you. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7, lower than the typical 3.2, played a similar supporting role in highlighting academic hurdles. On a brighter note, your high family income bracket nudged the prediction slightly toward success, as did your choice of full-time studies, which aligns with most students who pass—but these positive factors weren't strong enough to outweigh the others. Compared to typical students who get approved, your early and later school rankings stand out as much weaker, putting you in a tougher spot than most. This isn't a judgment on your potential or the barriers you've overcome as a Black woman in education; it's just the model weighing these metrics heavily. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later academic performance if possible, and highlighting any additional experiences that show your resilience—many reapply successfully after addressing key gaps like these. You've got this; keep pushing.
